data.title: Blackbaud: Price Baking In Strong Execution
data.description:
Introduction

Blackbaud (BLKB) has seen its share price fluctuate around the ~$60-70/share level for almost the past decade, with shares currently trading around $60, down ~14% year-to-date. Notwithstanding this year-to-date price decline, I believe that shares trade a little higher than I'd consider attractive enough to justify a buy. While I think sales will grow over time, macro, idiosyncratic funding risks, and M&A risk have me waiting for a lower price.

Sales: Near-Term Pricing Boost

Q1 sales came in at ~$281M, down ~2% from ~$286.6M in last year's Q3. If we adjust for the sale of EVERFI, however, sales actually grew ~5% organically, implying an organic revenue trend of ~$267M in sales last year to ~$281M today. I.e., EVERFI contributed ~$19M to quarterly sales last year.

On a reported basis, contractual revenue declined ~$12M to ~$183M (~6% decline), transaction revenue increased ~$8M to ~$93M (up 9%), and service revenue was down ~$1.4M to $5.3M (~20% decline). EVERFI was just over ~$18M of contractual sales, implying that, adjusted for this, sales grew organically across each of their segments except for Service sales. That is, after subtracting ~$18M from last year's contractual revenue, that segment would've reflected growth of ~$6M (~3-4%). Indeed, we know their Recurring revenue - contractual plus transactional - was up ~5.5% on an organic basis (and 6.1% year-to-date).

Jumping in with contractual sales, it's clear that price is driving a lot of the growth. From the 10-Q, they cited the "positive impact of our pricing initiatives" to their contractual revenue and also cited "positive results related to pricing initiatives" for their transactional revenue. Now, this is in addition to the positive "performance of our cloud solutions" and "increases in volume for our Blackbaud Integrated Payments", but I infer price to have been more material, something that's probably of no surprise to recent followers of Blackbaud.

For context, Blackbaud started a contract transition back in 2023 where they basically went to their customers with sub-3-year contracts and required them to either agree to a minimum 3-year (or longer) contract or essentially terminate their business, as they no longer agreed to deals less than 3 years. As the results reflect, most have agreed, and this shift has continued to benefit sales today.

Indeed, on the Q3 2024 call, Michael commented that they'll get through 90% of the transition by year-end. That is, they're not done transitioning contracts and have been transitioning contracts over the past 12 months. Indeed, in Q3 last year, they cited that by the "...end of 2024, approximately 65% of the eligible cohort, we have -- will have gone through the shift to modernize contract terms and pricing, leaving approximately 25% in 2025 and the last 10% in 2026."

Now, from an accounting perspective, when we contextualize sales growth year-over-year being attributable to price, that's a reflection of newly signed contracts at higher prices, not existing contracts. That is, for existing contracts, while they may have a price escalator built in per annum, from an accounting perspective, revenue is recognized pro-rata over the life of the contract, so revenue, in other words, recognizes a constant amount, not an escalating amount. Thus, when contextualizing contractual sales organically increasing ~$6M year-over-year, the price growth part reflects new or existing clients signing higher-priced contracts. And while I can't pin down the magnitude of price increases, something in the 3% range seems reasonable to me in terms of the sales benefit, or almost all of the contractual growth.

Now, they did nonetheless cite better performance of their cloud business, which seemingly entails new client wins on a net basis. They don't provide quarterly insight into the number of customers they have, but implicitly, there were offsetting trends here, which is what I'd expect. That is, as some customers entered the renewal phase, it's reasonable, in my opinion, to assume that they lost clients from proposed price increases that found better value propositions elsewhere. On the other hand, though, all you have to do is search "signed" in their past transcripts, and you'll find all sorts of new logo signings from Blackbaud happening quarter after quarter, so clearly, they're still winning new logos.

What the net of those trends was, it appears to be close to 0%. I mean, the rough math is that if ~25% of their contractual clients transitioned year-over-year and the TCV difference was ~10% thanks to embedded escalators, that would imply sales growth of around 3% from price alone, or roughly all of the year-over-year growth. They mention that some customers are actually signing 4-year (or longer) contracts, so the weighted average price is actually greater than 3% (as the TCV and thus pro-rated amount is greater). And they also said that they're "...doing more deals with multiple modules" - i.e., have been cross-selling existing clients - so it's very conceivable to me that their logo count was roughly unchanged year-over-year - if not marginally down - with the sales growth coming from higher sales per logo.

Contextualizing this logo trend, from an internal perspective, there hasn't been much going on. They've been adding some new AI capabilities to their products, but this more or less looks like just regular, day-to-day product improvements. And the same sort of goes for the macro situation - I don't see anything out there driving any material increase in the formation or spending of NFPs or other similar entities (churches, schools). It's possible that they've taken some share, although clearly, it wouldn't have been much. But to this point, Michael's previously commented about how many of their logo wins come from replacing outdated/legacy tools.

Their history largely supports this trend too. From 2015 to 2019, consolidated sales went from ~$638M to ~$900M, and then from there, there was roughly low-single-digit reported growth to ~$1.15B in 2024. Part of the pre-COVID growth was boosted by the YourCause and Reehler acquisitions, contributing ~$25M and ~$15M to sales, respectively (per my math) - excluding that and sales would have grown at an organic CAGR of around 7-8%. But then post-COVID, it's been a little slower - excluding the ~$75M in annual sales contributed by EVERFI and the CAGR would have been around 3% organically, adding ~$175M thereabouts in organic sales growth.

This isn't too bad, but it's not quite representative. Getting more specific by looking at contractual revenue, sales were $591M in FY20 and are on pace to be ~$725M today excluding EVERFI. Of this $130M in sales, however, price is likely more than half of it, implying about $60M in volume growth, or a 1-2% CAGR excluding price. So, with those results in mind and then layering on top the context above in terms of taking share from outdated solutions, I find it unlikely they've historically lost much, if any, structural market share. Inversely, I wouldn't really buy an argument that the market grew substantially, offsetting hypothesized share losses. As a cross-reference, Sylogist (OTCPK:SYZLF) also operates in this space, selling CRM software to NFPs, and we can see that Blackbaud hasn't seen worse sales growth than them.

Then lastly, I'll just point out that they've actually reduced headcount versus 2024, not increased, so any hypothesis that sales growth was affected by increased sales capacity would be disconfirmed. And to this end, if we just zoom out for a second, they ended FY22 with total headcount/sales reps of 3.2K/290, which declined to 3K/250 in FY23 and then again to 2.6K/210 in FY24, for which FY24 was partly impacted by the sale of EVERFI. This headcount trend essentially started in 2023 when the economy began softening, with management citing cost optimization as the driver, resulting in closing internal data centers and outsourcing certain in-house services and thus requiring less internal headcount.

Then in May last year (2024), they noted that they were "...going to continue to focus on trying to run the business with something close to our existing headcount." And this is mostly consistent with Michael's comments in February last year, noting that they "...don't see a substantial increase in the headcount," but "...do see significant opportunity, though, in productivity or quota attainment by person." So, given that context, it's unlikely there's any growth attributable to increased sales capacity.

On the transactional side of the business, sales were higher here organically (up ~9%), and we know here too there were price increases benefitting year-over-year sales, though we don't know how sizable they were. They did nonetheless cite some volume growth, however. As they've pointed out, while there were no "viral" events in the quarter prompting increased donation flow, volumes can be volatile, so it's tough to read too much into the growth, and indeed, they were expecting growth to slow here from the stronger first half (which was boosted by the LA wildfires at the start of 2025).

But from what I gather, this business has had minimal issues finding new customers and increasing volumes. Transactional sales were around the $210-215M mark in 2019 and 2020, which has subsequently grown to about $370M-ish here in 2025, part of which was a price similar to contractual sales.

Putting this all together, today's sales amount to about $1.125B on an annual run rate basis. Indeed, this is consistent with their guidance midpoint for FY25 revenue of $1.125B, implying Q4 sales of ~$292M, representing ~3% organic revenue growth after backing out EVERFI from last year's results.

All else equal, from a pricing perspective, they're going to continue to capture some growth. That is, based on the fact that I don't believe they've churned off many customers from recent price growth, I believe that (1) they can sustainably push through inflationary-like (2-3%) price increases for renewing customers that are already on 3-year contracts, and (2) capture the remaining benefits from contract transitioning in FY26. The latter may have something like an additional 2%-ish benefit next year, which should then stop benefiting results in FY27, but I would expect point 1 to sustain over time as a structural growth driver.

From a logo standpoint, though, we're not seeing much today, and I'm not anticipating seeing much going forward. I don't doubt that they'll take some share from outdated tech, but I also don't think it'll be of a material magnitude, consistent with their historical sales rep trend. That is, if there was a huge untapped market out there, I think there would be a clear sales rep growth trend and story reflecting this, but it's obvious there isn't. However, with relatively high retention rates and a decently full competitive landscape, I'm not anticipating share losses either.

Added all up - in addition to my assumption that transactional sales will grow over time - I'd be okay assuming 3.5% long-term sales growth for now between price and cross-selling. The main risks I see around this assumption are two-fold. For one, serving a lot of NFPs and other social-like organizations reliant on outside funding are subject to largely unpredictable funding shifts outside of Blackbaud's control. And this is outside of any uncontrollable changes in macro conditions, which could materially reduce something like transactional sales. And then for two, while I feel okay about the assumption, I'm not yet hugely confident in their long-term share trend given their post-COVID results and what that may mean, though I find it more likely than not they can maintain share and am willing to give them the benefit of the doubt.

Margins: Price Boosted

Q3 gross margins grew ~500 bps, growing from ~54.9% last year to ~59.7% this year. Adjusted EBITDA came in at nearly $100M, up nearly $5M from ~$95M last year, with margins expanding ~220 bps to 35.4% from ~33.2% last year.

On the gross margin front, you first have to adjust for EVERFI. From what I can find in the 10-Q, it looks to me like of the ~$19M in EVERFI revenue, there was about $7-8M in COGS associated with that, implying a gross margin of somewhere in the range of high-50%, right in line with their average. In other words, removed from their year-over-year results, we would've seen something like a ~$14M increase in sales and a ~$8M decrease in COGS on an organic basis, resulting in a ~$22M-ish increase in gross profit on an organic basis.

The reason for the dichotomous trend (increase in gross profit) seemingly stems primarily from 2 items. For one, a decent portion of the revenue growth had to do with just better pricing. And recalling the accounting treatment earlier for revenue, Blackbaud is essentially recognizing more revenue without an associated step up in cost. As such, practically all of that increase in reported revenue flowed straight through to gross profit, which could've alone accounted for about $10-14M of that gross profit increase. Then secondly, as was called out in the 10-Q, there was a nearly ~$8M reduction in impairment expense. Combined then, alongside some smaller savings elsewhere, that explains practically all of the organic gross profit (and margin) growth.

All of this growth obviously flows down to the operating level too and thus, correspondingly, boosts EBITDA margins. And specifically, given that the gross margin growth primarily stems from pricing and non-revenue-attached cost decreases, one would expect the vast majority of the ~500 bps gross margin improvement to flow down. Clearly, however, EBITDA margins grew by ~200 bps and not 500, so there's some offsetting cost growth (opex deleverage).

To this end, despite the reported sales decline of just over ~$5M (which includes the impact of EVERFI), their GAAP opex was down only around $3M, primarily from increases in G&A costs, which were offset by decreases in S&M, while R&D stayed flat. S&M's decrease was primarily attributable to the released EVERFI expenses - excluding EVERFI, S&M didn't change much, reflecting the uptick in opex leverage from an organic perspective (organic sales grew while S&M stayed flat). The G&A increase, however, largely had to do with expense allocation - from what I can gather, the sale of EVERFI resulted in material headcount adjustments in addition to their internal headcount changes, which consequently resulted in different allocation percentages leading to a larger G&A base.

So, we essentially have to look at this as a whole - that is, with R&D and S&M roughly flat organically, adding the ~$5M increase in G&A probably reflects a ~$5-6M increase in organic opex. Considering that sales grew ~$14M organically, that implies ~60%-ish incremental EBITDA margins. In other words, with the removal of EVERFI, it appears that EBITDA margins grew year-over-year.

And for further cost context, both COGS and opex have slightly benefited from recent footprint adjustments. As noted on the Q1 call, they've "...rightsized our operations by further rationalizing office leases and renegotiating significant vendor contracts," in addition to "...shutting down many of our private data at colos with only 2 of our own data centers remaining." The latter was not really in their results, having been completed in 2023, but the office lease buyout in February 2025 (driven by their remote-first strategy) did add a ~$2-3M benefit from lower rent expense per quarter.

Looking ahead, they're guiding for ~35.8% FY25 EBITDA margins, which implies ~$403M in EBITDA for the year, or ~$103M in Q4, representing a Q4 margin of 35.3%. I'm not thinking about there being a one-time-ish boost to margins akin to the impairment expense dynamic referenced earlier. However, with the continued transition to multi-year contracts, price increases should continue to benefit margins, which I estimate could be something like a 200-300-ish benefit in FY26, as essentially all of that flows to the bottom line.

Then from an opex standpoint, after a more eventful past 24 months around headcount and lease transitions, the next 24 months should be more stable. I referenced headcount earlier, but it sounds - and looks - as though they're fairly normalized (comfortable) at today's levels. And with the footprint rationalization behind them at this point - i.e., data center closures and lease terminations - I'm not really thinking about opex being leveraged via cost reductions.

Of course, beyond FY26, if they take a little more price than usual, that should lead to additional margin growth. But with my expectation/model for low-single-digit growth over time, I'm conservatively assuming that, with the cost savings behind them, after adding another 200 bps to FY26's margins to ~37.5%, they'll probably remain in that range.

Valuation

As one might expect, physical capex is fairly low for Blackbaud, spending around $5-10M annually, or about 0.6% of sales. However, there is a material amount of capitalized software spend - for the past 3 years, they've spent just about $60M in each year, or ~5.25% of sales. This is largely consistent with their FY25 capex guide too for ~$60M, including ~$55M of capitalized software costs.

As for the rest of the cash flow, it's been a mix of M&A and share repurchases. Without going into too much detail given the context of today's investment case, historical M&A-driven returns have been negative, mostly thanks to the EVERFI situation (which was bought for ~$744M in FY21 and going to be sold for a "nominal" amount). Summarily, they basically spent about $950M or so over the past 7 years, which has given shareholders fairly minimal return.

The key question is whether the future looks like the past? To this end, share repurchases have been materially ramped up lately, spending $418M in FY24 and another $133M year-to-date, and they do regard this as their number 1 use of capital going forward. On the other hand, they note that M&A is on the table as per their history.

So, does the increased prioritization of repurchases reflect a change of strategy? I don't know. Neither the Chairman nor CEO own a material amount of stock relative to their net worth at this point. But nonetheless, there's obviously an increase in the likelihood that repurchases continue for at least the next 12 months. However, I'm not so sure that there's enough here to make me confident that in 2027, I'm not going to wake up to Blackbaud completing a dilutive acquisition. So, I'll give them the benefit of the doubt here, but M&A risk is top of mind.

Putting the model together, I'm modeling FY26 sales and EBITDA at $1.164B and $436M, respectively (37.5% margin). Subtracting out stock comp at 10% of sales ($116M), D&A at 7% of sales also ($81M), interest of $60M, taxes at 25%, and then capex at $60M, I get to an annual run rate free-cash-flow figure of ~$147M.

This isn't too bad on today's market cap of ~$2.9B, implying a go-forward IRR of just about 8%-ish, which doesn't hugely change then whether they repurchase stock or not going forward (this model assumes no M&A). Could they bump that to low double digits? Perhaps so via successful price increases and cross-selling over time. However, at today's prices, I tend to see more average scenarios than huge upside.

For one, the macro's not guaranteed to improve nor be conducive going forward. For two, while their competitive position is fairly durable, it doesn't appear to be a lock over time. And then thirdly and lastly, capital allocation is rather mixed, although more likely than not, they'll be repurchasing shares going forward. So, given what I think is the general economic picture here, the investment is sort of just "meh"... nothing overly exciting, but nothing overly bad.

Conclusion

Ultimately, Blackbaud is certainly a business I'd be open to owning, but just not at this price. The business has a number of fundamentally attractive elements to it, but the structural capital allocation risk involved in the story weighed against the implicit IRR noted above have me demanding a lower price.

data.title: EWO: Valuations Still Look Cheap, But Probably Prudent To Book Profits Now
data.description:
Introduction

The iShares MSCI Austria ETF (EWO) is a passively managed ETF (it tracks the MSCI Austria IMI 25/50 Index) that covers 20 stocks from the Austrian equity markets. We last reviewed EWO in April, when he fancied its prospects and had assigned a buy rating on the same. EWO has gone on to deliver the goods, generating total returns of 35% in 7 months and outperforming other Eurozone stocks (as represented by the iShares MSCI Eurozone ETF), and global stocks (as represented by the Vanguard Total World Stock ETF) by 1.46x and 1.30x, respectively.

Is EWO still worth holding on to after this sturdy run, or would it be more sensible to take some profits now? Well, let’s break down and review some of the key narratives that could weigh on the prospects of this product.

Steady GDP Progress, But Inflation Risks And Waning Consumption Appetite Don’t Bode Well

Austria which had long been beset by declining GDP growth from 2023 to the second half of 2024, is now showing gradual signs of an improvement, with positive GDP growth seen in the last 4 quarters.

However, at the end of the day, investors should still be aware that this is an economy that is poised to grow at just one-fifth the pace of Europe by the end of the current year, and only half the pace of Europe in 2026.

We are also not too chuffed to note that private consumption momentum (which accounts for more than half of Austrian GDP), which was in a healthy space, appears to have reversed quite dramatically over the last two months. After seeing healthy positive growth previously, retail sales have been declining for the last couple of months.

Diminished purchasing sentiment may also have something to do with how inflation, which appeared to be quite subdued, is now consistently coming in at 4% levels.

Banking Sector Isn’t The Most Resilient, But Erste Group Is Faring Well

If EWO is to continue to flourish, we suspect much will depend on its banking cohort (this sector alone accounts for 48% of the portfolio), and one banking stock in particular- Erste Group Bank AG (OTCPK:EBKDY), which alone accounts for one-fourth of the portfolio.

One of the big risks that Austrian banks could face is the risk stemming from their exposure to CRE [Commercial Real Estate] loans, which is believed to be one of the highest in Europe.

So far this year, higher impairments and higher provisioning for Austrian banks appear to be capping net profit progression, which is down on a YoY basis.

EBKDY, on the other hand, is in better shape as it is more diversified and exposed to the fast-growing economies of Central Europe. It also helps that EBKDY management is doing a great job in keeping a lid on costs. Take, for instance, the cost-to-income ratio, which is a mark of cost efficiency in the banking sector; this ratio has been on a consistent decline, and is now below their target range of 48%.

EWO’s Sturdy Dividend Yield No Longer In Play

When we last recommended EWO, it was yielding an outstanding dividend figure of almost 7% annualized, which was well above what it normally yields. However, the combination of steep price appreciation and a cut in EWO’s June distribution by around 13% YoY has meant that the yield is now roughly only on par with what EWO has traditionally yielded on average (around 4.3-4.4%).

Once again, EBKDY’s dividend shift has likely left a mark here. Earlier in the year, EBKDY sought to take a 49% stake in Santander Bank Polska S.A.(OTCPK:BKZHF) by shelling out EUR 7B, and to fund that, it needed to cut its dividend payout ratio for 2025 to 10% (whereas the previous dividend payout policy was 40-50%). This appears to be only a temporary cut, as next year they plan to go back to their old policy, although much of the payouts will also depend on how effectively EBKDY can grow its profit base.

Valuations Still Look Compelling

One of the main reasons why we posited a buy rating on EWO was that its valuations looked inordinately cheap relative to Europe and the globe, more so for the degree of long-term earnings growth it was offering. Now note that despite EWO’s strong appreciation over the past 7 months, that compelling valuation differential still exists, while its long-term earnings growth potential has expanded even further (back then EWO was set to deliver long-term earnings of high-single digits; now it is in the double-digit terrain).

While EWO’s P/E valuation multiple has expanded by 9%, it is still only priced at less than 10x, which represents a 46-48% discount over global stocks and Eurozone stocks. Crucially, at much pricier P/E multiples, these other diversified pockets also offer lower earnings growth than EWO.

Strong Momentum, But Risk-Reward For A Long Play Looks Less Appealing Now

If you’re someone who’s guided by the principles of momentum, you’ll be interested to note that EWO still continues to tick quite a few boxes.

On the daily chart, EWO continues to witness a series of higher-highs (HHs) and higher lows (HLs), and is also trading above its 3 key moving averages of 50, 100 and 200

However, to get a wider perspective, let’s also look at the price movements on the larger time frame- the monthly chart. Here we can see that from 2009 until May this year, EWO has been progressing within the boundaries of a faint ascending channel (marked by the two black lines). EWO finally broke out of this 16-year channel in July, which may be construed to be a major technical event. However, do consider that the volumes associated with this breakout have been relatively subdued, and even though the price continues to trend higher, the associated volumes have only dipped.

Also take note of the RSI indicator at the lower end of the chart, which is now on the cusp of hitting the overbought zone. The last time the RSI hit such steep levels was back in early 2018, after which EWO experienced a pronounced downtrend and correction that lasted over two years.

We are not suggesting that EWO will see a correction of the same magnitude, but we also don’t feel it would pay to be too intrepid at these levels and continue to buy. EWO has also now witnessed 11 straight green-bodied candles (where the monthly close has been higher than the monthly open), and we feel a pullback of some sort is long overdue.

Another ancillary reason not to get too exuberant about EWO now is because, quite unlike the mean-reversion opportunity in early 2024, Austrian stock’s relative strength [RS] ratio versus Eurozone stocks has already more than normalized to its long-term mean and is now trading at a slight premium.

To conclude, we are revising our rating on EWO from a buy to a hold.

data.title: Brookfield Asset Management Q3: Achieved Record-High Earnings (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I rate Brookfield Asset Management Ltd. (BAM, BAM:CA) a Buy, for capital appreciation-focused investors who are looking for long-term buy and hold investments. This global alternative asset manager just announced Q3 earnings, achieving record-high earnings, and $30 billion in capital raise. The investor deck projects the company will double its business in the next five years, from a combination of client growth, investment growth, and the significant growth potential of the alternatives market, including infrastructure, private equity, private credit, real estate, and renewable power. The stock has outperformed the sector, achieving a high level of total return.

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the stock are:

  • A dividend yield of 3.2% paid quarterly, goes ex-dividend 11/28.
  • $1 Trillion in assets under management (AUM)
  • Participation in the fast-growing alternatives market, expected to double within the next 5 years from $25 trillion to $60 trillion in 2032.
  • Strong liquidity of $2.6 billion with an investment grade rating of A / A- with Fitch and S&P.
  • Senior management team with deep experience in each asset class.

Note, Seeking Alpha Quant rates the stock a Sell 2.39 (as of 11/13/25) due to its premium price. Note the D- in valuation, while the other metrics, such as profitability (A) and growth (B+) are strong. As of 11/13/25, the stock dropped -5% to $51.87 amid analyst price targets in the $70s, representing future upside.

Global Alternative Asset Manager

Headquartered in New York, Brookfield Asset Management was incorporated in 2022 and operates as a subsidiary of Brookfield Corporation. With over $1 Trillion in assets under management, BAM is a leading global alternative asset manager, invested across renewable power & transition, infrastructure, private equity, real estate, and credit. The company’s objective is to generate attractive, long-term risk-adjusted returns for the benefit of clients and shareholders.

BAM manages a range of public and private investment products and services for institutional and retail clients. The company earns fee income for its work, while also co-investing in the same investments as clients, thus ensuring an alignment of interests. BAM’s sheer scale in capital raising and deployment of capital gives them a competitive advantage that few can match.

The chart below shows how BAM raises capital from institutional, insurance, private wealth, and public markets to deploy into their 5 main business segments.

Q3 2025 Earnings Results

On November 7, the company declared Q3 earnings with the following financial highlights:

  • Fee related earnings went up +17% year-over-year to a record $754 million.
  • Distributable earnings up +7% to $661 million.
  • Net income up +33% to $724 million.
  • $30 billion capital raised in the third quarter.
  • Announced agreement to acquire remaining interest in Oaktree Capital Management.
  • Launched an AI infrastructure fund.

Overall, the third-quarter results were exceptional, in my view. The company announced multiple record highs in earnings and fundraising, which creates momentum for the future. They also announced the final acquisition of Oaktree, which is a prized asset further cementing brand recognition, especially with those investing in retirement accounts.

Performance To Date

BAM has delivered a strong internal rate of returns (IRR) across their 5 main business segments, of which Private Equity is the strongest, with 20% net IRR.

An investment of $10,000 in BAM at IPO in 2022 would be worth $18,800 today, outperforming the Financial Sector (XLF).

Valuation

BAM is premium priced vs. the sector. Its price/earnings ratio of 31.36 represents a 168% increase over the sector of 11.68. However, it is common in my experience for leaders in the sector to warrant a premium price. Price to book is similarly premium at 9.31 on a trailing 12-month basis, vs. 1.18 for the sector. Again, looking to future earnings growth from alternative investments, equity is expected to grow at a significant rate.

Since going public in 2022, BAM has paid a consistent, steadily rising dividend. Total distributions were $1.28 in 2023, $1.52 in 2024, and an estimated $1.76 in 2025.

Competition

In a comparison to peers, BAM stands out for having the second-best price performance year to date, by only declining -4%. It also has the second-highest price earnings ratio at 31.4, along with the second-highest return on equity of 22.6%. This says the stock is selling at a premium; however, it is generating a return on capital invested.

Blackstone Inc. (BX) is the largest alternative asset manager and has a more recognizable name than Brookfield. Its 5-year total return is 200%, and its 10-year is 607%, which means it tripled over the past 5 years.

KKR & Co. Inc. (KKR) is trading at the highest premium with a P/E of 56.7 and generated a substantial 10-year total return of 711%.

Ares Management Corporation (ARES) delivered a remarkable 1,344% total 10-year return. As an alternative manager, they have a very strong name and popular funds discussed on Seeking Alpha, such as the business development companies ("BDCs") Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC) and Ares Dynamic Credit Allocation Fund (ARDC).

The Carlyle Group (CG) is making a rebound since the last time I wrote about this peer group. Year to date, its price is up 5.8%, which is the only stock to grow in the sector. Its 5-year and 10-year total returns are a respectable 128% and 389%, respectively.

Outlook

The growth potential of alternative markets has grown significantly in recent years, especially with the change in law to allow retirement accounts, a $12 trillion market. BAM is exploiting these opportunities as a leader in the space, capitalizing on their scale, leveraging existing clients, and utilizing research to unlock extraordinary opportunities.

BAM projects the alternative business to grow from $25 trillion today to $60 trillion by 2032. Key growth drivers are global AUM growth, institutional investors allocating more to alternatives, and investors consolidating accounts to the winning largest and most diversified asset managers such as BAM. More and more, sophisticated investors are increasing capital allocation to alternatives because they generate excess returns, add diversification, reduce volatility, and can create predictable cash flow.

Q4 results will be reported February 10, 2026.

Risks To Rating

As the Dow and S&P 500 (SP500) have just reached record highs, analysts are concerned the market is ripe for a correction, especially those stocks that are tied to AI. Therefore, a reasonable risk to BAM is being viewed as part of the AI infrastructure, which could turn negative very quickly, in my view. Given the diversification in their portfolio and long-term durable capital positioning, a major correction would pose an opportunity to buy shares at a discount.

Bottom Line

I rate Brookfield Asset Management a Buy based on the company’s momentum, along with its business model that justifies a high valuation. BAM’s third quarter was characterized by achieving record highs in earnings (+17%), capital raises ($30 billion) and closing key projects such as the final acquisition of Oaktree and the establishment of an AI infrastructure fund.

BAM pays a quarterly dividend yielding 3.2%, going ex-dividend on November 28th. Since the IPO, the company has consistently raised the dividend each year. While the stock is not an income play per se, it is more of a long-term capital appreciation strategy. However, the dividend serves to anchor the valuation given any market volatility.

The company is well managed in my view, with senior managers who have been in place for 20 years or longer, resulting in institutional expertise. BAM currently manages $1 trillion in AUM, which is expected to double in the next five years.

data.title: Comcast: 3 Reasons The Stock Is Flashing A Buy Sign Right Now
data.description:
So far, the market rally in 2025 has been absolutely dominated by high multiple tech names, a market move that has largely left traditional 'cash cow' businesses overlooked. As investors rotate into momentum plays, legacy firms with stable business models have largely fallen by the wayside.

One of these names is Comcast (CMCSA), an impressively large media and cable conglomerate that many perceive as being 'stuck in the past'.

As cord cutting has accelerated in recent years - driven by the rise of internet enabled media disruptors - Comcast's vertically integrated business model has faced headwinds from all directions.

Competition from Charter (CHTR) and secular subscriber losses in cable and home phone have many investors questioning the core economic engine of the business, and new competition on the media side from platforms like Netflix (NFLX) and Amazon Prime (AMZN) has put pressure on CMCSA's historically dominant media brands.

On the surface, Comcast appears to be a melting ice cube destined for the dustbin of history.

However, when you dig a layer deeper, you'll find that the firm has done a mostly successful job pivoting into a new digital age.

Despite that, shares currently trade at an unbelievably cheap looking 6.4x earnings.

Today, we'll explore Comcast's prospects, outline our thesis, and highlight three key reasons we believe CMCSA is currently an attractive looking 'Buy' in today's market.

Sound good? Let's dive in.

Reason 1: Organic Business Stability

As we just mentioned, it appears as though the common investor narrative around Comcast has to do with consumer cord cutting and the firm's retail cable losses.

Historically, this has been an incredibly important segment for the company, so it makes sense that as media distribution has largely moved to the Internet, legacy infrastructure like cable has been left behind.

That said, Comcast, and its core connectivity business, Xfinity, have pivoted successfully to serving customers - in the majority - with home & mobile Internet.

You can see this below, with CMCSA's total internet revenue next to the red arrow, and total cable revenue next to the orange arrow:

While the internet business (in and of itself) is currently undergoing some consolidation as competition from Charter and regional players heats up, management has argued that, long-term, they believe it will become a two-player market. We agree. Few regional players have the capital advantages of Comcast, and so we view subscriber volume losses, which remain small nominally, as a short-term issue:

So starting with convergence, the broadband environment remains intensely competitive, which we do not expect to change anytime soon. Over time, though, we believe that the vast majority of the broadband market will be comprised of 2 multi-gig symmetrical providers serving most addresses, and we aim to be a winner in this segment, with the rest of the market likely being served by capacity limited alternatives.

We've been seeing this end state begin to take shape. Fiber expansion continues at a steady pace. And as we've said before, we expect most of our footprint will eventually be overbuilt. At the same time, fixed wireless remains a durable competitor, serving price-sensitive segments with moderate performance needs.

True, consumers have been active in cutting their cable video subscriptions, but we don't see home or mobile Internet going anywhere.

At the same time, revenue from cable TV packages has been shrinking as a piece of the overall pie, which means that going forward, these cable TV losses will be less and less relevant to the investor narrative.

In Q3 of 2025, cable TV accounted for $6.6 billion of Comcast's $31.2 billion in overall revenue, representing less than a quarter of the company's overall business.

Not only that, but as consumers have transitioned towards a subscription service model for their media - primarily with providers like Netflix, Amazon Prime, Disney+, et cetera - many have found that the combined costs of those subscriptions appear relatively similar to a traditional cable TV package. We expect that as time goes on, 'Subscription Fatigue' (which this dynamic has been dubbed) may actually stem cable TV losses, as consumers paying one consolidated bill may choose to keep their current setup.

Other than these cable losses, Comcast is a stable, growing business with growing home and business Internet segments, a growing media business (when you adjust for the Paris Olympics), and Studios / theme parks segments that have grown YoY:

Again, if you remove the Olympics, Comcast revenue actually grew YoY, which may surprise some.

These numbers are not the numbers of a business in terminal decline, but rather a legacy firm that has managed to pivot successfully into the modern era.

We believe that this transition has gone underappreciated by the market, which brings us to reason number two.

Reason 2: The Outrageously Cheap Valuation

As we just mentioned, shares of Comcast appear incredibly cheap right now, trading in a blended price-earnings ratio of only 6.5x.

Over the next few years, analysts are expecting earnings to grow in the mid-single-digit range through the end of the decade:

This earnings-per-share growth is expected to come through a stable revenue base - as we just discussed - along with increasing efficiencies on the product side, along with continued share buybacks:

That said, while average earnings-per-share growth estimates remain modestly positive over the next few years, 2025 and 2026 are expected to be down years, which we expect is the core reason shares of CMCSA are down so significantly over the last year:

However, a ~40% sell-off due to two years of -2% EPS growth appears overdone in our opinion.

Looking historically, since 2018, Comcast has averaged an EPS growth rate of 6.39%, driving an implied fair value of roughly 15x earnings. Historically, the stock has traded at an average earnings multiple of roughly 12.8x earnings.

Between these two figures, we can apply an average fair value of roughly 13.5x earnings:

However, as we mentioned, shares of Comcast are currently trading at 6.5x, which implies 100%+ potential upside in the stock simply through multiple expansion as business 'gets right'.

Bake in low to mid single-digit earnings-per-share growth over the next five years, and the total returns case here becomes quite compelling.

Broadly, this undervaluation reminds us of the considerable trough that tobacco companies went through over the last five years, as fears around regulatory tightening and the decline of the sticks business kept share prices deflated.

However, as we called out, these businesses (like British American Tobacco) were too cheap and have since rallied massively:

As another business with a perceived 'terminal decline' problem and an incredibly cheap multiple, we think Comcast is in a similar position and could rally significantly in the coming years.

Reason 3: The Dividend

Finally, in addition to Comcast's stable core business and incredible discount, investors now also get paid to wait with an incredibly robust, well backed dividend that is nearing a 5% yield.

Traditionally, Comcast has not been known as a high dividend payer, but it has been a consistent payer, with 16 consecutive years of dividend growth:

Now, with the share price decline, we've seen the yield increase to more than 4.8%—nearly 5% - which adds on yet another total return stream and pays investors to wait for a revaluation that is more in line with Comcast's actual business potential.

From a dividend safety perspective, Comcast only pays out a low 20s percentage of its free cash flow and earnings, meaning that we expect Comcast will be able to support the dividend at its current level on an ongoing basis:

Finally, when you look at the long term, Comcast has seen 'yield on cost' increase tremendously over the last 30 years, indicating the company is paying the consistent, growing dividend through real economic growth as opposed to short-term moves that may harm long-term-oriented investors:

It's an attractive, ~5% dividend, and when combined with valuation upside and stable, low-single-digit EPS growth, we believe that there's a strong total returns package here for investors, which informs our 'Buy' rating.

Risks

Of course, there are some risks here as well for Comcast investors.

The primary concern with Comcast is competition both on the infrastructure side and the media side.

On the infrastructure side, Spectrum is owned by Charter Communications, and remains a key national competitor. Comcast's Xfinity brand does not operate in a monopoly-style market, and we see margins getting worse before they get better as competition from Optimum (formerly Altice) (ATUS), Google Fiber, and other regional players continues to rage.

At the same time, Comcast has done well with its media performance in recent years, but its Peacock streaming service remains an 'also ran' competitor in a highly competitive media industry dominated by YouTube (GOOG), Netflix, and Amazon.

The growth here has been solid, but we think it's going to be a very long time before Peacock generates any material profitability.

Additionally, while cable represents a relatively small portion of Comcast's revenue. The connectivity segment produces a lion's share of CMCSA's profit, implying that continued cable losses may weigh more heavily on earnings going forward.

We don't expect this will threaten the dividend, but it may put a headwind on organic EPS growth, which could harm our overall thesis.

Finally, Comcast's stock - frankly - has been trading quite poorly from a momentum perspective. In the stock market, momentum tends to be get momentum, and we expect that share price declines could continue at least in the short term until positive catalysts significantly change investor perception.

Until then, it's possible that shares will drift around aimlessly without any direction, and quantitative investors that use momentum as an input may stay away from the stock.

Summary

Overall, we believe that Comcast shares present a compelling opportunity.

The business is largely stable and even expected to grow modestly in coming years. Not only that, but it trades at a significant discount to historical measures and the market more broadly.

Finally, the firm's well backed - nearly 5% - dividend, promises to reward patient investors even as negative momentum persists.

As a result, we rate Comcast a 'Buy'.

Cheers~

data.title: Private Markets: Why Retail Investors Should Stay Away
data.description:
By Sebastien Canderle

As regulators move to open private markets to a wider investor base, the question is not whether retail access should be allowed, but whether the structure of these markets can support it. Illiquidity, opaque performance reporting, and misaligned incentives between fund managers and investors already challenge institutional participants.

With fee structures built for scale and governance mechanisms that provide limited accountability, extending the model to smaller investors risks amplifying those weaknesses rather than democratizing opportunity.

New legislation seeks to grant retail investors universal access to private capital. In August, the Trump Administration issued an executive order entitled “Democratizing Access to Alternative Assets for 401(k) Investors.”[1]

European authorities are not to be outdone. The British government has set the minimum to invest in long-term asset funds[2] as low as £10,000. The European Union’s Long-Term Investment Fund[3] product imposes no minimum.

While illiquid or so-called “semi-liquid” private markets are now accessible for most retail investors, participating without understanding their limits could prove costly.

Hazy Performance and Poor Liquidity

Assessing the true performance of private markets is difficult. Reported returns are often opaque and cannot be precisely benchmarked.[4] The illiquid nature of these investments compounds the problem. Although private capital funds are typically structured with 10-year maturities, few distribute capital on schedule.

A Palico analysis of 200 private equity (PE) funds found that more than 85% failed to return investors’ capital within that timeframe, and many successful venture funds take over a decade to reach a successful exit.[5]

Secondary markets offer limited relief. While investors can sell stakes, transactions are sporadic and frequently completed at a discount to net asset value. The scale is also tiny compared with public markets: secondary trading represents less than 5% of the primary market in PE,[6] and less than 1% in private credit.[7] Once committed, investors cannot easily exit, and pricing transparency is minimal.

The opacity endemic to private markets also raises a crucial question about performance. Whereas, on average, 1990s and early 2000s PE vintage funds did consistently deliver better returns than those of public markets, in the face of a massive inflow of capital allocated to the sector, outperformance has dwindled for recent vintages.

Overallocation led to market saturation in developed economies,[8] inflating asset valuations and making it harder for fund managers to derive any sustainable angle, consistently and persistently, to beat their peers or even public markets.

Performance Erosion

Market saturation has steadily lowered performance targets in PE. Typical internal rate of return (IRR) goals have declined from about 25% in 2000 to roughly 15% today. To offset this, some firms have reduced or removed the traditional 8% hurdle rate and raised their share of capital gains above the historical 20% level, ensuring manager compensation is maintained even as returns compress.

The industry’s profit engine has shifted from investment returns to asset accumulation. Large managers now channel more capital into scalable, lower-return strategies such as private credit and infrastructure.

Apollo (APO) manages roughly $700 billion in private credit compared with $150 billion in PE, for instance. In other words, fund managers prioritize their own over their clients’ profitability. Management and advisory fees at Blackstone (BX) have exceeded performance fees in seven of the past 10 fiscal years, a pattern echoed across the sector.

Unsurprisingly, recent 401(k) products offered by private capital firms to retail investors follow the same model, emphasizing predictable credit and real estate exposures rather than potentially higher-return but more competitive PE and VC.[9] With competition for deals intensifying, scale - not performance - has become the more reliable path to profitability.[10] And the focus for alternative asset managers to fundraising, even if it means moving away from their core competency.[11]

Opacity Invites Audacity

Eager to grow assets under management, private capital firms are actively lobbying governments and legislators to deregulate further.[12] This is a risky proposition.

In the market euphoria that preceded the global financial crisis, private markets were the subject of numerous cases of alleged corruption and collusion, with regulators imposing heavy fines on several of the largest PE groups.[13]

Beside the risk of fraudulent and questionable activity, private markets’ illiquid and opaque nature makes it hard for investors to gauge the competence of individual fund managers.

In the UK, for instance, Neil Woodford, a seasoned asset manager in public equity, proved a poor allocator of funds across various private market asset classes.[14] Many of his PE and venture holdings underperformed, leading to the collapse of Woodford Equity Income in 2019, after that investment vehicle had lost over £5 billion in value.

What should concern prospective retail investors further is the pervasiveness of agency problems in private markets. The asset management trade is primarily focused on the fund manager’s controls[15] and economics[16].

This default modus operandi, coupled with the lack of accountability and deficient supervision, contributes to a skewed outcome in favor of the fund manager.

Institutional Failure

Institutional limited partners (LPs) accept many of private markets’ inefficiencies because they too manage other people’s money. Pension funds, insurers, and endowments charge their own fees and often benefit from the same layering of costs (via multiple layers of fees)[17] that inflates fund managers’ earnings. As a result, few institutional investors are motivated to curb those practices.

Oversight mechanisms are also weak. Replacing an underperforming or unethical general partner (GP) typically requires approval from 75% of investors – a high hurdle that leaves most managers entrenched.

Meanwhile, personal and professional ties between LP executives and PE firms further blur accountability. Many senior LP representatives sit on advisory boards or attend networking events hosted by the GPs they are meant to oversee, creating subtle but powerful conflicts of interest.

In theory, LP investors should hold private capital fund managers to the same fiduciary standards that the latter apply to their portfolio companies. In practice, the balance of power tilts heavily toward fund managers, a structural flaw that perpetuates weak governance and limited investor protection.

If Too Small to Play, Stay Away

Institutional investors have realized their lack of influence in reining in the worst behaviors of fund managers and become more aware of the excessive remuneration that these fund managers draw in relation to their actual performance.

Some of the larger LP investors — including pension fund managers like BlackRock (BLK) and Canada Pension Plan, Singapore’s sovereign fund GIC, and Australian bank Macquarie (OTCPK:MCQEF) - have scaled back commitments to external fund managers and chosen to build in-house alternative asset management divisions.

In turn, private capital fund managers have looked for other sources of funds. The largest ones derive perpetual capital from in-house insurance vehicles.[18] It eliminates the need to go to market regularly to raise fresh funds. But perpetual capital pools are only one provenance of easy money.

Taking the retail route is another valuable avenue. One less demanding than institutional LPs. No retail investor could request an observer seat on the advisory board of a private capital firm.

None would ever get sufficient influence to challenge the level of commissions. None will have the wherewithal to monitor or investigate a fund manager’s investment decisions. They will be forced to rely on brokers and other intermediaries, piling on further commissions and agency problems.

Retail investors are likely to be even more accommodating than institutions when facing a hike in carried interest or the removal of hurdle rates. In short, they offer all the benefits of institutional money without many of the inconveniences.

As a recent report by PitchBook stated about the opportunity to commit to private markets, “For some allocators, the added complexity and illiquidity will be justified by diversification and alpha potential; for others, staying in public markets may prove the more appropriate path.”[19]

Until private capital faces stronger oversight and offers better terms as far as fees and capital gain allocation are concerned, as well as more liquid secondary markets, retail investors would be better served remaining in public markets.

[1] Trump's 401(k) executive order marks big changes for retirement savings - and possibly puts your money at risk

[2] What Is a Long-Term Asset Fund?

[3] European Long-Term Investment Fund (ELTIF)

[4] Myths of Private Equity Performance: Part IV - CFA Institute Enterprising Investor

[5] Venture Capital: Lessons from the Dot-Com Days - CFA Institute Enterprising Investor

[6] The Evolution of the Private Equity Secondary Market - CAIS

[7] Unpacking private credit secondaries | Macfarlanes Private Capital Solutions

[8] Private Equity: Market Saturation Spawns Runaway Dealmaking - CFA Institute Enterprising Investor

[9] PitchBook Analyst Note: The New Face of Private Markets in Your 401(k)

[10] New Breed of Private Capital Firms Will Face Performance Headwinds - CFA Institute Enterprising Investor

[11] New Breed of Private Capital Firms Will Face Performance Headwinds - CFA Institute Enterprising Investor

[12] Apollo's Marc Rowan says Europe 'at war with itself' over finance regulation

[13] The Good, the Bad and the Ugly of Private Equity: Success and Failure in Buyout Land

[14] FCA fines Neil Woodford and his company £46mn for management failures

[15] The Private Capital Wealth Equation, Part 1: The Controls Variable - CFA Institute Enterprising Investor

[16] The Private Capital Wealth Equation, Part 2: The Economics Variable - CFA Institute Enterprising Investor

[17] Agency Capitalism in Private Markets: Who Watches the Agents? - CFA Institute Enterprising Investor

[18] Permanent Capital: The Holy Grail of Private Markets - CFA Institute Enterprising Investor

[19] Allocator Solutions: Are Private Markets Worth It?

Disclaimer: Please note that the content of this site should not be construed as investment advice, nor do the opinions expressed necessarily reflect the views of CFA Institute.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Coterra Energy: Flexible Multi-Basin Oil And Gas Producer With Strong Free Cash Flow Generation
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Coterra Energy (CTRA) is an oil and gas producer with high-quality multi-basin assets across the US, with a stock that’s been relatively flat since their big deal with Cimarex Energy in late 2021, when they also renamed themselves Coterra.

Right now, CTRA seems to offer a good opportunity thanks to the attractive valuation, with natural gas and increased production thanks to recent deals leading to significant cash flows despite lower oil prices.

The company boasts their high-quality assets, with significant well cost reductions in all their basins since 2023 (21% for the company as a whole), low reinvestments, and an average inventory duration of ~15 years across their 3 basins. This multi-basin focus allows them to diversify their exposure and commodities, with the natural gas focus helping them withstand the oil fall recently and even leading to an increased guidance, while they can focus their investment efforts on whatever opportunity they find - wherever it is.

As they also show, this flexibility allows them to deliver resilient free cash flows in several environments, taking advantage of higher oil or gas prices when the other one falls. Despite this, we’ve also seen activist investor Kimmeridge saying in an open letter that Coterra’s board should appoint an independent, non-executive chair and refocus operations on its oil-rich Delaware Basin and avoid the “complexity, inefficiency, and valuation compression,” pointing out they trade below both Permian and gas-focused peers, as CTRA also shows.

The firm says Coterra should divest its Marcellus and Anadarko assets to become a pure-play Permian company, so basically the opposite of the current focus (as they actually boosted the Marcellus investment program by $7 million vs the February guidance).

Compared to peers, including three that I own and some very good ones, we see a very solid break-even, which is ultimately an advantage we see reflected even in their Q3 report, which came with very solid cash flows despite the falling oil prices.

Regarding the free cash flow, it came in at around $533 million (adjusted), expecting ~$2 billion in 2025 despite the significant ~$2.31 billion CAPEX expected in 2025, backed by solid natural gas prices and higher production expectations. This is assuming WTI at ~$60 and HH at ~$4.00, so it’s not some crazy expectation, and the previous guidance was assuming WTI at ~$71 and HH at $4.22, delivering $2.7 billion in FCF, so there’s definitely strong potential.

Financially, based on CTRA’s latest report, we see a good position, with little cash now but significant additions to their properties, with a manageable amount of debt. We see a significant increase in equity since Q4’24, with the acquisitions of certain assets from Franklin Mountain Energy Holdings LP and Avant Natural Resources LLC to boost their Permian assets, at a combined value of nearly $4 billion (~$1 billion in stock), expecting these to be more than 15% accretive to free cash flows per share in 2025 to 2027.

As we can see, their debt maturities are well-spread into the future, with quite low-cost debt and a history of conservative net leverage (currently at 0.8x vs 0.3x at the end of Q4’24). Still, we can see the longer-term debt coming at higher interest rates due to the current environment, which can also be an opportunity if they decide to refinance them again once interest rates are cut some more.

Dividends & Buybacks

Coterra pays a ~3.33% dividend yield, estimated to cost them ~$672 million in 2025, with modest buybacks that they’re restarting now in Q4 2025 and being “opportunistic in purchasing our shares” - with the extra FCF being planned for debt reduction and share repurchases. This year, they’re opting for $600 million in debt repayments over buybacks, while last year they spent ~$451 million for that instead, with minimal debt repayments.

Risks & Opportunities

Perhaps the most important opportunity for oil comes from OPEC and its allies recently agreeing to boost their oil production in December by the same amount as in October and November but deciding to pause its increases in Q1 2026, at least. These hikes were key in oil’s fall this year, as OPEC’s moves are ultimately one of the most important factors to keep in mind, so a potential reversal to the levels before these hikes has the potential of being a significant catalyst for the price of oil.

In my recent coverage of TotalEnergies (TTE), we’ve also seen a massive change in their annual energy outlook report, where they now estimate a new peak in oil demand that’s a whole decade further than last year’s expectations. There are several factors they name here, such as the slowdown in EV adoption following several moves, including lower incentives, Trump’s LNG permitting policies (also good for CTRA), and more, which is in line with the common trend we now see in oil giants, slowing down their investments in green ambitions and reverting to their reliable oil and gas operations.

Also, why I believe the pure-play focus on oil while selling their natural gas assets could be a mistake is because LNG still has massive potential, especially with a huge pipeline of projects becoming operational in the following years and Trump’s $750 billion US-EU LNG deal mentioned before. In such an environment, we could possibly even be looking at a revaluation of these assets just from this catalyst alone, especially if there’s a supply gap due to lower investments in production caused by lower oil prices today, as we’ve seen many oil companies do recently.

Meanwhile, another very important potential catalyst came from Trump’s sanctions on Russian oil, and TotalEnergies’ CEO even called them very important recently and even mentioned how the world is underestimating their impact, highlighting how premiums are rising as countries seek alternative shipment routes.

Again, these are the same sanctions that almost instantly pushed Chinese state-owned companies to halt their Russian oil imports and also made other Chinese oil buyers reduce their Russian oil purchases, which is a huge blow to Russia that can have significant effects on the future of their war in Ukraine, while they also forced Lukoil to sell its international assets shortly after these sanctions came, which is seen as the most consequential action on a Russian company since the war.

Continued US rate cuts can also be a significant catalyst to keep in mind, with a new one in October and some talks and expectations for a December cut. This can be great for oil stocks from several points of view, including boosting the demand for oil by increasing economic activity and decreasing their interest expenses ($206 million TTM) while also potentially fueling CTRA’s expansion through cheaper debt.

Still, these things take time, and right now we’ve been looking at the US Government going through what recently became its largest shutdown in history, causing the BLS to halt all data collection and publishing during it. As a result, the Fed has less up-to-date data during a time when I believe the impact of tariffs to the economy could’ve been clearer. And again, we’ve seen Powell citing some worries about inflation and unemployment back at the October meeting, so the timing was actually bad for this catalyst.

Another important factor to pay attention to, especially when we’re talking about an oil company, is Trump’s relationship with Venezuela, which can rapidly deteriorate once again (see 2018 Venezuelan elections and 2019 recognition of opposition leader Juan Guaidó as interim president), and even though Trump stated that he’s not planning any war with the country, he also said that Maduro’s days are numbered, so this will remain something that can change overnight.

Again, Venezuela is not just some random country with oil. The country is known to have the world's largest proven oil reserves - meaning roughly 4 times more than the US or Russia, or nearly 2 times the two combined! This comes with massive geopolitical importance, as Venezuela is one of the 5 founding members of OPEC, which would very likely not just sit and watch the US take over (with or without a war). Any issues here can lead to significant disruption in global oil trade (which can lead to higher prices), similar to or even more intense than wars in other regions relevant for oil, and I believe this can potentially be something of a magnitude we haven’t even seen before when it comes to oil prices.

So, although there are quite a few catalysts that can improve CTRA’s future, there are also near-term headwinds to keep in mind, especially related to the country’s economy and political uncertainty. Their management will have to navigate this environment carefully and should take a careful approach in balancing taking advantage of the potential opportunities without deteriorating their balance sheet at a time when the near-term future of rate cuts is under pressure, although I believe the long-term is still in their favor.

Peer Comparison

Compared to peers such as Devon Energy Corporation (DVN), APA Corporation (APA), Chord Energy Corporation (CHRD), Diamondback Energy (FANG), EQT Corporation (EQT), Occidental Petroleum Corporation (OXY) and Permian Resources Corporation (PR) - many of which I already covered and rated positively (3 of which I actually own) - we see that they are not really the best in terms of most valuation metrics, with other peers coming at significantly more attractive P/CF or PB ratios, for example.

But still, we’re looking at relatively low ratios generally speaking, especially when comparing them to larger US peers.

Valuation

For CTRA’s valuation, starting by annualizing 2025’s FCF and getting to $1.8 billion (vs the $2 billion expected that adjusts the changes in assets and liabilities), let’s assume a 4.5% CAGR for the first 5 years thanks to the accretion of the recent acquisitions, rate cuts advancing and potential for other expansion projects, Big Beautiful Bill benefits, and support by natural gas and LNG’s potential, followed by a 3.25% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.25% terminal growth rate. Assuming a WACC of 9% (estimated ~8.93% based on a cost of equity of ~10% and ~4.75% cost of debt), as well as $3.82 billion in net debt and 767 million shares outstanding. This way, we get to an enterprise value of $30.09 billion, an equity value of $26.27 billion, and an intrinsic value of $34.25 per share.

Conclusions

I rate CTRA a Buy thanks to their solid fundamentals, clear undervaluation, and great asset base with low-cost break-evens, although there are better peers in today’s market. There are risks associated with the macro environment and their industry, but CTRA remains a solid option to keep in mind for high-quality, relatively low-leverage oil and gas multi-basin exposure.
